exemplar.title: To what extent can the Bradshaw Model be applied in the river Ewenny?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent can the Bradshaw Model be applied in the river Ewenny?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with explicit Bradshaw model links and testable hypotheses.
Geographic context precisely defined with river reach length, county borders and feasible data-collection sites.
Multiple author-drawn maps follow cartographic conventions and situate data-collection locations effectively.
weaknesses: One regional map lacks a scale bar and coordinate grid, reducing locational clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission meets all band-3 descriptors: the question is well formulated and linked to theory, context is highly specific, and mapping is clear. The minor map omission does not detract from its overall excellence in Criterion A, meriting the full 3 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods for width/depth, velocity and load size are clearly described with diagrams and linked to hypotheses.
Data-collection techniques (tape measure, float test, pebble sampling) were correctly applied at all sites.
weaknesses: Explanations of sample-size choices and timing are brief and lack statistical justification.
No discussion of secondary data sources and limited commentary on measurement reliability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The write-up appropriately describes and implements relevant field methods (band 2), but explanations remain succinct and do not achieve comprehensive theoretical alignment or full reliability justification, warranting 2 marks.

strengths: All primary data directly address the research hypotheses, with a variety of appropriate presentation techniques (line graphs, box plots, scatter plots).
Figures are titled, numbered and sourced, supporting data interpretation.
Overall data sufficiency enables meaningful analysis.
weaknesses: Occasional convention errors (duplicate figure numbers, missing axis units, inconsistent superscripts).
Trend lines lack statistical significance metrics (p-values, confidence intervals).
Box plots omit outlier notation and explicit whisker definitions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The information is generally well treated, relevant and sufficiently presented, placing it in the 3â4 band. Given the competent use of multiple presentation forms and reasonable data sufficiencyâdespite some lapsesâan upperâband score of 4 is justified.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive statistics and Pearsonâs r are used appropriately, and anomalies are linked to human factors and theory.
Trends and anomalies are interpreted in relation to the research question and Bradshaw model.
Analysis addresses both hypotheses with clear data-theory connections.
weaknesses: No confidence intervals for hydraulic radius and misinterpretation of correlation significance.
Incomplete discussion of null-hypothesis acceptance implications.
Some gaps in quantitative robustness (e.g., anomaly quantification).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis demonstrates clear descriptive and statistical techniques with coherent data-theory linkage, aligning with the 3â4 band. While lacking full statistical rigour and completeness, its strengths in interpretation justify 4 marks.

strengths: A clear conclusion is presented, summarizing main findings.
weaknesses: Overstates support for hypotheses despite statistical insignificance.
Fails to acknowledge key limitations and nuances in model applicability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion restates results but provides only partial evidence linkage and omits discussion of statistical insignificance, fitting the 1-mark band.

strengths: Multiple relevant limitations are identified with discussion of their impacts (e.g., scale mismatch, float reliability).
Improvement suggestions (larger river reach, flowmeter use, seasonal repeats) are pertinent to data reliability.
weaknesses: Suggestions lack detailed feasibility analysis and quantification of expected impact.
Evaluation does not fully explore resource or time constraints.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation comprehensively outlines relevant strengths, weaknesses and improvement ideas. Although it could deepen feasibility and impact discussion, it meets the criteria for the top band of evaluation, justifying 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two author-drawn maps include north arrow, scale bar, colour key and site symbols; a third map spatially locates bar charts.  Although stylistically simple, these satisfy cartographic conventions and clearly situate the investigation.

The research question is clearly phrased, explicitly geographical and solidly anchored in the Bradshaw model, a syllabus-relevant theory.  Two measurable hypotheses are derived from the question, demonstrating that the formulation enables hypothesis testing.

A mixture of line graphs, box-whisker plots, scatter plots and GIS maps is used; most are appropriate and aid interpretation.  Some graphs lack units on axes or have duplicated figure numbers, showing competent but not âmost effectiveâ presentation.

Tape-measure, float test and systematic pebble sampling are appropriate and were applied at 20 sites, but the write-up concedes measurement inaccuracy and limited spatial coverage.  Data quantity is therefore moderate, fulfilling the strand descriptor for correct but not high-quality implementation.

Descriptive statistics and Pearsonâs r are applied; anomalies are explained qualitatively.  No confidence limits for hydraulic radius are provided and significance of r is misinterpreted, so techniques are appropriate but lack rigour required for the 5-6 or 7-8 bands.

Trends and several anomalies are linked to human modification, tributary input and Bradshaw expectations, demonstrating clear though not exhaustive linkage between data, question and theory.  Depth of explanation is adequate for the 3-4 range.

The student specifies a 16 km reach of the River Ewenny, names the adjoining counties, and identifies four precise data-collection locations with five stratified sites each.  The description and accompanying text show that primary data collection on site was feasible and indeed undertaken.
Three key limitations are identified and their impacts discussed with some specificity (e.g., scale mismatch, float reliability).  Coverage is sound but not exhaustive, meriting the middle band.

Primary datasets (hydraulic radius, velocity, load size) directly address both hypotheses and are generally sufficient, yet variables such as gradient or dischargeârelevant to the Bradshaw modelâare absent.  Relevance is good but not exhaustive, matching the mid-band.

The conclusion restates results but fails to acknowledge statistical insignificance and overstates support for hypotheses.  Evidence linkage is therefore partial rather than full, fitting the 1-mark band.

Both hypotheses are analysed, but the investigation omits other model variables and contradicts itself (accepting null yet claiming hypothesis proven).  Thus, analysis shows noticeable but not crippling gaps, aligning with the 3-4 descriptor.

Methods for width/depth, velocity and load size are described with diagrams and rationale, linking each technique to the hypotheses.  However, explanations remain brief (e.g., no justification of sample size choice or timing) and omit discussion of secondary data, so coverage is adequate rather than comprehensive.

Figures have titles, numbered captions and referenced sources.  Minor convention errors (two Fig. 13s, inconsistent superscripts) appear, yet overall adherence is reasonable with only occasional lapses, consistent with the 3-4 mark band.

Suggested improvements (larger river, flowmeter, seasonal repeat) are relevant and linked to reliability, yet explanations lack detailed consideration of feasibility or likely magnitude of impact, corresponding to band 2 rather than 3.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical, anchored in the Bradshaw model, and enables the formulation of testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Map of South West Wales lacks scale bar and coordinate grid. Include these to align with cartographic conventions and improve locational clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Justify the choice of five sites per location by explaining how this sample size supports statistical reliability of results.
Present the hydraulic radius formula in LaTeX as $$R=\frac{A}{P}$$ and specify units (mÂ² for area, m for perimeter) for clarity.
Geographic context is well described with river reach length and county border, enabling clear site feasibility for primary data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Quantify anomalies by reporting deviation from trend line values (e.g., percentage difference) to strengthen analysis robustness.
Box-whisker plot lacks outlier notation and explicit whisker definitions. Add these and axis scale to improve presentation clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Include a scale indicator (e.g., a metre rule) in width measurement images to validate the technique and improve reproducibility.
Provide rationale for collecting ten depth readings per transect, perhaps based on desired confidence in mean depth estimates.
Discuss the representativeness of pebble samples: randomize sample points within the transect to reduce selection bias.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Clarify interpretation thresholds for Pearsonâs \(r\) (e.g., weak, moderate, strong) and explain how they will inform hypothesis testing.
Graph axes lack units. Add âmÂ²â for cross-sectional area and âmâ for wetted perimeter to comply with presentation conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The analysis links anomalies to human modifications effectively, demonstrating clear data interpretation and theory integration.
Duplicate figure number âFig.13â appears. Renumber consistently to avoid confusion and ensure accurate cross-references.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Trend line on velocity graph is shown without significance metrics. Include p-value or confidence intervals to validate the trendâs statistical strength.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Graph is mislabelled in text as âFigure 21â but appears to be Fig. 18. Ensure caption numbers and in-text references match.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Although you note p = 0.834, there is no discussion of what accepting the null hypothesis implies for your research question; add interpretation.
Show full substitution in the correlation equation to guide readers through each calculation step: $$r=\frac{-1.485}{\sqrt{0.756\times1146.556}}$$.
The conclusion overstates hypothesis support despite statistical insignificance for velocity; temper claims and acknowledge limitations.
Revise the final âto what extentâ statement by integrating both hypotheses and anomalies, reflecting a nuanced model applicability.
Table improvement suggestions lack feasibility detail (resources, time) and expected impact magnitude; expand to deepen evaluation.
Detail how seasonal repeats could quantify variability (e.g., Â±10% discharge change) to strengthen evaluation depth and relevance.
Hypotheses are clearly derived from the Bradshaw model, providing measurable predictions directly linked to theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Rationale for the second hypothesis includes general erosion definitions; focus more tightly on hydrodynamic effects on load size.
Map of the River Ewenny is author-created with key and scale, effectively situating data-collection sites.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context
